title: Dog Penis Swollen and Stuck Outside Sheath: Paraphimosis Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog's penis stuck outside the sheath? This is a medical emergency known as paraphimosis. Learn the signs, risks, and when to see a vet.
content_html:
Understanding Paraphimosis in Dogs

It can be very distressing to notice that your dog's penis is swollen, discolored, or unable to retract back into its protective sheath, known as the prepuce. This condition is medically referred to as paraphimosis. It occurs when the penis becomes trapped outside the sheath, often due to hair entanglement, a narrow opening, or persistent swelling after arousal.

Is This a Medical Emergency?

Yes, paraphimosis is a high-priority medical emergency. When the penis remains outside the prepuce, the tissue quickly becomes dry, irritated, and swollen. This swelling can further restrict blood flow, acting like a tourniquet. If the blood supply is cut off for too long, it can lead to permanent tissue damage, infection, or necrosis. If your dog's penis has been stuck for more than 20 minutes, you must seek veterinary care immediately.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If you are contacting an emergency clinic or a telehealth service, providing a clear photograph of the affected area is incredibly beneficial. A photo allows the veterinary team to assess the color of the tissue—ranging from healthy pink to concerning shades of purple or black—and the severity of the swelling. This visual information helps them prioritize your case and provide immediate instructions while you are en route to the clinic.

faqs.question: Can I try to fix it at home?
faqs.answer: You can try applying a water-based lubricant or a cool (not ice-cold) water compress to reduce swelling while you prepare to leave. However, never force the tissue back into the sheath, as this can cause further injury.

faqs.question: What are the common causes of paraphimosis?
faqs.answer: Common causes include long hair at the tip of the sheath getting caught, a naturally narrow preputial opening, trauma during mating, or neurological conditions.

faqs.question: What will the veterinarian do?
faqs.answer: The vet will likely clean and lubricate the area, use medications to reduce swelling, and gently guide the penis back into the sheath. In some cases, a small surgical incision or sedation may be required to resolve the issue.
